title: ABAP Unit Testing & TDD
description: ABAP Unit testing and Test-Driven Development are not optional at Staff+ level — they are the primary mechanisms for building quality into a system that cannot be rewritten. In a 20-year-old codebase with millions of lines of ABAP, your ability to add tests to legacy code determines your velocity. This section covers the technical mechanics and the strategic tradeoffs.

sections.title: ABAP Unit Test Framework

description.value:
ABAP Unit is the built-in unit testing framework in SAP NetWeaver. It is modeled after xUnit patterns (JUnit, NUnit) and integrates with the ABAP Workbench. A test class is defined with the FOR TESTING addition, which excludes it from production runtime. Risk levels (HARMLESS, DANGEROUS, CRITICAL) determine whether tests can run in a production system.

Test vs Test-Including Class: ABAP Unit supports two approaches: (1) a test-including class — the test class is local to the production class (using the DEFERRED and INCLUDE pattern), meaning tests are in the same include as the production code. (2) a test class that is separate — the test class is in a different include, referencing the production class via DEFINITION DEFERRED. The separate approach is preferred for larger systems because production ASHOST does not load test includes.

Coverage: ABAP Unit integrates with the SAP Coverage Analyzer (transaction SCOV). Each test run records which statements and branches were executed. The coverage data is stored in the system and can be aggregated across runs. Coverage is not a goal in itself — it is a signal. Use coverage to find untested code, not to enforce arbitrary thresholds.

sections.codeBlock:
@EndUserText.label: 'Basic ABAP Unit Test Class'
CLASS zcl_order_calculator DEFINITION PUBLIC FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS calculate_discount
      IMPORTING
        iv_order_total  TYPE i
        iv_customer_tier TYPE string
      RETURNING
        VALUE(rv_discount) TYPE i.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_order_calculator IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD calculate_discount.
    IF iv_customer_tier = 'GOLD'.
      rv_discount = iv_order_total * 20 / 100.
    ELSEIF iv_customer_tier = 'SILVER'.
      rv_discount = iv_order_total * 10 / 100.
    ELSE.
      rv_discount = 0.
    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

" === TEST CLASS (local, FOR TESTING) ===
CLASS ltcl_order_calculator DEFINITION FOR TESTING
  RISK LEVEL HARMLESS
  DURATION SHORT.

  PRIVATE SECTION.
    DATA mo_calculator TYPE REF TO zcl_order_calculator.

    METHODS setup.
    METHODS test_gold_discount FOR TESTING.
    METHODS test_silver_discount FOR TESTING.
    METHODS test_no_discount FOR TESTING.
    METHODS test_edge_zero FOR TESTING.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS ltcl_order_calculator IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD setup.
    mo_calculator = NEW zcl_order_calculator( ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD test_gold_discount.
    DATA(lv_discount) = mo_calculator->calculate_discount(
      iv_order_total    = 1000
      iv_customer_tier  = 'GOLD' ).
    cl_abap_unit_assert=>assert_equals(
      act = lv_discount
      exp = 200 ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD test_silver_discount.
    DATA(lv_discount) = mo_calculator->calculate_discount(
      iv_order_total    = 1000
      iv_customer_tier  = 'SILVER' ).
    cl_abap_unit_assert=>assert_equals(
      act = lv_discount
      exp = 100 ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD test_no_discount.
    DATA(lv_discount) = mo_calculator->calculate_discount(
      iv_order_total    = 1000
      iv_customer_tier  = 'BASIC' ).
    cl_abap_unit_assert=>assert_equals(
      act = lv_discount
      exp = 0 ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD test_edge_zero.
    DATA(lv_discount) = mo_calculator->calculate_discount(
      iv_order_total    = 0
      iv_customer_tier  = 'GOLD' ).
    cl_abap_unit_assert=>assert_equals(
      act = lv_discount
      exp = 0 ).
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

decisionTree.prompt: Should I write a unit test for this method?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Does the method have conditional logic, calculations, or data transformations?" If yes → unit test. If no (pure delegation, single RFC call) → integration test may be more appropriate.

branches.condition: Method contains business logic (calculations, conditionals, branching)
branches.path: Write unit test. Focus on: (a) each branch of every IF/ELSE/CASE, (b) boundary values (EDGE), (c) error paths (cx exceptions), (d) data transformations. Aim for 5-8 assertions per method.

branches.condition: Method is a pure delegation (passes input to another method/RFC with no transformation)
branches.path: No unit test needed. Cover via integration test that validates the full chain. Unit testing delegation adds test maintenance cost with zero value.

branches.condition: Legacy method with no DI, hard-coded DB access or RFC calls
branches.path: Write a characterization test (golden master). Run the method with known inputs, record outputs, then refactor to enable DI. Only then write proper unit tests with test doubles.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: Unit test the hard stuff — complex logic, calculations, data transformations. Don't test getters, setters, or delegation. Every test is a maintenance liability; make each one earn its keep by catching real bugs.

sections.title: Test Doubles & Dependency Injection

description.value:
Dependency injection is the architectural enabler for testability. In classic ABAP, classes directly instantiate their dependencies via CREATE OBJECT or call static methods (CALL METHOD class=>static_method). This creates hard-coded dependencies that cannot be replaced with test doubles. The solution is to pass dependencies via the constructor or a setter.

ABAP Test Double Framework: SAP provides cl_abap_testdouble for creating test doubles. The framework supports mocks (behavior verification), stubs (return canned data), and test doubles (record-and-replay). For ABAP Unit, the preferred approach is using the test double framework over hand-written mock classes because the framework maintains the contract automatically.

Constructor Injection Pattern: Pass all external dependencies (database access classes, BAPI wrappers, HTTP clients) via the constructor. Store them in instance attributes (mo_db_access, mo_api_client). The production code creates real implementations. The test code injects test doubles. This is the same pattern used in Java Spring, C# DI containers, and Python unittest.mock.

Limits of Test Doubles: Not everything should be doubled. Database access that involves complex WHERE conditions and JOINs is better tested in integration. The test double for a BAPI should validate that you pass the right parameters, not that the BAPI processes them correctly. Know the boundary: test doubles for your code, real implementations for third-party code.

sections.codeBlock:
@EndUserText.label: 'Test Double Definition + Constructor Injection'
" === Interface for external dependency ===
INTERFACE zif_pricing_api.
  PUBLIC.
  METHODS get_price
    IMPORTING
      iv_product_id  TYPE string
      iv_quantity    TYPE i
    RETURNING
      VALUE(rv_price) TYPE i.
ENDINTERFACE.

" === Production class with constructor injection ===
CLASS zcl_order_pricing DEFINITION PUBLIC FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS constructor
      IMPORTING
        io_pricing_api TYPE REF TO zif_pricing_api.
    METHODS calculate_order_total
      IMPORTING
        iv_product_id  TYPE string
        iv_quantity    TYPE i
      RETURNING
        VALUE(rv_total) TYPE i.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
    DATA mo_pricing_api TYPE REF TO zif_pricing_api.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_order_pricing IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD constructor.
    mo_pricing_api = io_pricing_api.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD calculate_order_total.
    DATA(lv_unit_price) = mo_pricing_api->get_price(
      iv_product_id = iv_product_id
      iv_quantity   = 1 ).
    rv_total = lv_unit_price * iv_quantity.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

" === Test class with test double ===
CLASS ltcl_pricing_test DEFINITION FOR TESTING
  RISK LEVEL HARMLESS DURATION SHORT.

  PRIVATE SECTION.
    DATA mo_pricing_double TYPE REF TO zif_pricing_api.
    DATA mo_cut TYPE REF TO zcl_order_pricing.

    METHODS setup.
    METHODS test_calculates_total FOR TESTING.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS ltcl_pricing_test IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD setup.
    " Create test double for interface
    mo_pricing_double ?= cl_abap_testdouble=>create(
      'zif_pricing_api' ).

    " Configure stub behavior: price = 100 per unit
    cl_abap_testdouble=>configure_call(
      )->returning( 100 ).
    mo_pricing_double->get_price(
      iv_product_id = 'PROD-001'
      iv_quantity   = 1 ).

    " Inject double via constructor
    mo_cut = NEW zcl_order_pricing( mo_pricing_double ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD test_calculates_total.
    DATA(lv_total) = mo_cut->calculate_order_total(
      iv_product_id = 'PROD-001'
      iv_quantity   = 5 ).

    cl_abap_unit_assert=>assert_equals(
      act = lv_total
      exp = 500 ).  " 5 × 100

    " Verify the API was called exactly once
    cl_abap_testdouble=>verify_expectations( ).
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

quantifiedTradeoff.title: Hand-Written Doubles vs Test Double Framework

hand_written.writeLatency: 4 hours per mock class (first time), 1 hour maintenance per change
hand_written.monthlyPenalty: $2,400/month — each interface change requires updating the mock. Mock drift (out-of-sync with interface) causes false test failures.

test_double_framework.writeLatency: 30 min per configuration, zero maintenance on interface changes
test_double_framework.monthlyPenalty: $300/month — framework automatically adapts to interface changes. No drift. No mock update on refactor.

calculation.breakeven: The test double framework wins at 1+ method changes per quarter. For stable interfaces (changed less than once every 6 months), hand-written doubles are acceptable but still not recommended.

sections.title: TDD in ABAP

description.value:
Test-Driven Development in ABAP follows the same Red/Green/Refactor cycle as any other language. The discipline is the same: (1) write a test for the behavior you want, (2) watch it fail (red), (3) write the minimal code to pass (green), (4) improve the design without changing behavior (refactor). The difference is the tooling and the legacy context.

TDD in a Legacy System: You cannot practice pure TDD on a 20-year-old function group with 5,000 lines and no tests. The approach is: (1) write a characterization test (golden master) for the existing behavior, (2) refactor the code to extract testable units, (3) write new tests for the extracted code using TDD, (4) replace the old code call with the new tested code. This is the seam pattern from Feathers' Working Effectively with Legacy Code.

Writing Tests Before Code — The Discipline: The hardest part of TDD is writing the test first. It forces you to think about the API design before implementation. If writing the test is awkward, the API is poorly designed. If the test requires complex setup, the class has too many dependencies. TDD is not about testing — it's about design. The test is a secondary benefit.

sections.codeBlock:
@EndUserText.label: 'TDD Cycle Example — Test First'
" === STEP 1: RED — Write the test first ===
CLASS ltcl_discount_tdd DEFINITION FOR TESTING
  RISK LEVEL HARMLESS DURATION SHORT.

  PRIVATE SECTION.
    METHODS test_gold_tier_20_percent FOR TESTING.
    METHODS test_silver_tier_10_percent FOR TESTING.
    METHODS test_basic_tier_zero FOR TESTING.
    METHODS test_max_cap_500 FOR TESTING.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS ltcl_discount_tdd IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD test_gold_tier_20_percent.
    " This will fail — class ZCL_DISCOUNT doesn't exist yet
    DATA(lo_cut) = NEW zcl_discount( ).
    DATA(lv_result) = lo_cut->calculate(
      iv_amount      = 1000
      iv_tier        = 'GOLD' ).
    cl_abap_unit_assert=>assert_equals(
      act = lv_result
      exp = 200 ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD test_silver_tier_10_percent.
    DATA(lo_cut) = NEW zcl_discount( ).
    DATA(lv_result) = lo_cut->calculate(
      iv_amount      = 1000
      iv_tier        = 'SILVER' ).
    cl_abap_unit_assert=>assert_equals(
      act = lv_result
      exp = 100 ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD test_basic_tier_zero.
    DATA(lo_cut) = NEW zcl_discount( ).
    DATA(lv_result) = lo_cut->calculate(
      iv_amount      = 1000
      iv_tier        = 'BASIC' ).
    cl_abap_unit_assert=>assert_equals(
      act = lv_result
      exp = 0 ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD test_max_cap_500.
    DATA(lo_cut) = NEW zcl_discount( ).
    DATA(lv_result) = lo_cut->calculate(
      iv_amount      = 10000
      iv_tier        = 'GOLD' ).
    cl_abap_unit_assert=>assert_equals(
      act = lv_result
      exp = 500 ).  " 20% of 10000 = 2000, capped at 500
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

" === STEP 2: GREEN — Write minimal implementation ===
CLASS zcl_discount DEFINITION PUBLIC FINAL CREATE PUBLIC.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS calculate
      IMPORTING
        iv_amount      TYPE i
        iv_tier        TYPE string
      RETURNING
        VALUE(rv_discount) TYPE i.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_discount IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD calculate.
    DATA(lv_pct) = SWITCH i(
      iv_tier
      WHEN 'GOLD'   THEN 20
      WHEN 'SILVER' THEN 10
      ELSE 0 ).
    DATA(lv_raw) = iv_amount * lv_pct / 100.
    rv_discount = nmin( val1 = lv_raw val2 = 500 ).
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

" === STEP 3: REFACTOR — Improve without changing tests ===
" Extract magic numbers to constants.
" Add input validation.
" Extract cap calculation to separate method.

adversarialResponse.challenge: "TDD in ABAP is impractical. SAP projects have tight deadlines, legacy code has no tests, and writing tests first doubles development time."
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "You have time to fix bugs but not time to write tests that prevent them." (preachy, dismissive of context)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "I hear the practical concern — TDD does have an upfront cost, especially in a legacy SAP environment. Let me break down the math: (1) For new code, TDD adds 20-30% to initial development time but reduces bug-fix time by 50-70%. Over the first 6 months, you break even. Over 12 months, you're ahead by 30%. (2) For legacy code, you don't TDD the function group. You extract a small class (the seam), write a characterization test, then TDD the new behavior. The investment is ~2 hours per seam, and the return is a testable API that survives refactoring. (3) The key insight: TDD is not about testing — it's about design. Every time you find the test awkward to write, you discover a design problem before it becomes production code. The 20-30% overhead is design insurance. (4) Start small: one method, one test, one week. Measure: defect rate in that code drops. Scale from there. I've seen teams adopt TDD in ABAP by starting with a single new development and expanding by 10% each sprint."
adversarialResponse.pattern: quantified-argument — acknowledge the cost concern, provide concrete data, differentiate new vs legacy code, reframe TDD as design tool, propose incremental adoption with measurement.

sections.title: Test Coverage & Code Quality

description.value:
Test coverage measurement in SAP uses the Coverage Analyzer (transaction SCOV). It tracks statement coverage (every ABAP statement), branch coverage (every IF/ELSEIF/ELSE/CASE/WHEN path), and condition coverage (every boolean sub-expression in complex conditions). The data is stored per user, per system, and can be aggregated by package and by development class.

Coverage Thresholds: Blind thresholds are dangerous. A team that enforces "80% coverage minimum" will discover that the last 20% of code is 80% of the work (the Pareto principle applies to testing too). Better approach: set different thresholds for different code categories. Business logic (calculations, validations, pricing) should target 90%+ branch coverage. Error handling and logging should target 60%+ statement coverage. User interface (ALV, selection screens) should not have coverage targets — test UI through integration tests.

Coverage-Driven Development: Use coverage data to drive your testing strategy, not as a compliance metric. When a module's coverage is low, that is a risk signal — prioritize testing that module based on its business criticality and change frequency. The Coverage Analyzer can show coverage trends over time: is coverage going up (good) or down (bad) for each package?

sections.codeBlock:
@EndUserText.label: 'Coverage Measurement via SCOV'
" === SAP Coverage Analyzer (SCOV) ===
" Transaction: SCOV
"
" Coverage run:
"   1. SCOV -> Goto -> Coverage Run -> Create
"   2. Enter: Run name: RUN_Q4_2024
"   3. Scope: Package Z_SALES_ORDER
"   4. Activate coverage measurement
"   5. Execute ABAP Unit tests (transaction SE80 or via CI)
"   6. Deactivate measurement
"   7. SCOV -> Evaluate -> Display results
"
" Result per object:
"    Object              | Statements | Branches | Risk
"    ZCL_ORDER_CALC      | 92%        | 88%      | LOW
"    ZCL_PRICING_ENGINE  | 45%        | 30%      | HIGH
"    ZCL_LOGGER          | 15%        | 10%      | OK (utility)

" Programmatic coverage query
CLASS zcl_coverage_report DEFINITION PUBLIC FINAL CREATE PUBLIC.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS get_package_coverage
      IMPORTING
        iv_package        TYPE devclass
      RETURNING
        VALUE(rv_stmt_pct) TYPE i.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_coverage_report IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD get_package_coverage.
    " Query coverage data via function module
    CALL FUNCTION 'SCOV_GET_COVERAGE'
      EXPORTING
        iv_package = iv_package
      IMPORTING
        ev_stmt_coverage = rv_stmt_pct.

    IF rv_stmt_pct < 60.
      " Log warning to CI dashboard
      DATA(lo_logger) = zcl_devops_log=>get_instance( ).
      lo_logger->log_warning(
        iv_message = |Coverage for { iv_package } is { rv_stmt_pct }%| ).
    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

" CI pipeline gate:
"   IF coverage < 80% -> pipeline WARNING
"   IF coverage < 50% -> pipeline FAIL (new code must have tests)
"   IF coverage drops by >5% vs previous run -> pipeline FAIL

quantifiedTradeoff.title: Coverage vs Effort — Where to Invest

calculation.formula: EffortToTarget = CurrentGap × ComplexityFactor, ROI = DefectPrevention / Effort

high_value_code.writeLatency: 4 weeks to cover critical paths (20% of code, 80% of defects)
high_value_code.monthlyPenalty: $12K investment. Prevents ~70% of production defects. Payback: 2 months.

all_code_uniformly.writeLatency: 12 months to reach 80% across all packages
all_code_uniformly.monthlyPenalty: $180K investment. Defect reduction plateaus at 50% coverage. Last 30% of coverage costs 70% of effort.

calculation.breakeven: Target the critical 20% first: pricing, billing, order calculation, integration APIs. These are 80% of production incidents. Defer utility classes, logging, and rarely-changed reports. Reassess quarterly.

sections.title: Integration Testing for SAP

description.value:
The test pyramid for ABAP has the same shape as any other system: many unit tests at the bottom, fewer integration tests in the middle, and a handful of end-to-end tests at the top. However, ABAP has a unique challenge: the double-testing problem. Because ABAP logic often involves database access, RFC calls, and cloud APIs, developers tend to test the same business logic at multiple layers — once at the unit level and again at the integration level. This creates maintenance overhead without proportional quality benefit.

Sealed Book Principle: Once you have tested a piece of business logic at the unit level (with test doubles), do not re-test the same logic at the integration level. The integration test should verify that the wiring is correct — that the RFC call works, that the database connection is configured, that the OData service is reachable. Not that the discount calculation produces the right value — that was already tested.

Integration Test Subjects: (1) Database interaction — SELECT, UPDATE, MODIFY operations with real or in-memory HANA databases. (2) Remote communication — RFC calls, BAPI invocations, SOAP/REST services. (3) CDS view integration — that a view compiles and returns data when consumed. (4) OData service — that the service definition is valid and returns the expected structure. (5) Authorization — that DCL rules are enforced correctly.

sections.codeBlock:
@EndUserText.label: 'Integration Test with Test Doubles'
" === Integration test for CDS view consumption ===
CLASS ltcl_cds_integration DEFINITION FOR TESTING
  RISK LEVEL HARMLESS DURATION MEDIUM.

  PRIVATE SECTION.
    DATA mo_env TYPE REF TO if_cds_test_environment.

    METHODS setup.
    METHODS teardown.
    METHODS test_view_returns_data FOR TESTING.
    METHODS test_view_respects_dcl FOR TESTING.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS ltcl_cds_integration IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD setup.
    " Create CDS test double environment
    mo_env = cl_cds_test_environment=>create(
      iv_view_name = 'Z_SALES_ORDER_VIEW' ).

    " Insert test data
    mo_env->insert_test_data(
      iv_table_name = 'vbak'
      it_data = VALUE vbak_tab(
        ( vbeln = '001' kunnr = 'C001' netwr = '1000' waerk = 'USD' )
        ( vbeln = '002' kunnr = 'C002' netwr = '2000' waerk = 'USD' ) ) ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD teardown.
    mo_env->destroy( ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD test_view_returns_data.
    " Execute the CDS view
    SELECT * FROM Z_SALES_ORDER_VIEW
      WHERE SalesOrder = '001'
      INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_result).

    cl_abap_unit_assert=>assert_not_initial(
      act = lt_result ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD test_view_respects_dcl.
    " ... authorization test with mock user
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

" === RFC integration test ===
CLASS ltcl_rfc_integration DEFINITION FOR TESTING
  RISK LEVEL HARMLESS DURATION MEDIUM.

  PRIVATE SECTION.
    METHODS test_bapi_call FOR TESTING.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS ltcl_rfc_integration IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD test_bapi_call.
    " Call BAPI_FLIGHTS_GETLIST in test system
    CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_FLIGHTS_GETLIST'
      DESTINATION 'NONE'
      EXPORTING
        airline = 'LH'
      TABLES
        flight_list = DATA(lt_flights)
        return      = DATA(lt_return).

    cl_abap_unit_assert=>assert_not_initial(
      act = lt_flights
      msg = 'BAPI should return flights' ).
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

comparisonTable.title: Test Types for ABAP — Scope, Speed, Confidence

criteria.unit_test: Single class, single method, isolated with doubles
criteria.integration_test: Component boundary — DB, RFC, CDS, OData
criteria.system_test: Full scenario — Fiori → OData → CDS → DB → response

criteria.unit_test: Logic correctness — high for specific method
criteria.integration_test: Wiring correctness — medium for component boundary

criteria.unit_test: Low — double contract binds to interface
criteria.integration_test: Medium — test data changes with schema
criteria.system_test: High — UI changes break tests frequently

comparisonTable.staffPlusTip: ABAP-specific: keep unit tests HARMLESS so they can run in production. Keep integration tests DANGEROUS so they only run in DEV/QAS. Never run system tests in PRD.

sections.title: Behavior-Driven Development (BDD) with ABAP

description.value:
Behavior-Driven Development (BDD) brings the language of business stakeholders into the testing process. Instead of writing technical ABAP Unit tests that verify implementation details, BDD tests describe behavior in natural language using the Gherkin syntax: Given (initial context), When (action), Then (expected outcome). The tests become executable specifications that product owners can read.

Cucumber for SAP: The open-source project abap-cucumber (or the SAP BDC / ecatt automation) allows Gherkin feature files to be executed against ABAP systems. Each step in the Gherkin file maps to an ABAP method in a step definition class. The framework parses the feature file, matches steps to definitions, and executes the ABAP code, reporting pass/fail for each scenario.

Why BDD for SAP: The gap between business requirements and technical implementation is wider in SAP than in most ecosystems. Business users understand "When I create a sales order for a gold customer, I get a 20% discount." They do not understand "IF iv_tier = 'GOLD' AND iv_order_total > 1000..." BDD bridges this gap by making the tests themselves the requirements.

Organizational Impact: BDD shifts the testing conversation from "what does the code do?" to "what should the behavior be?" This is powerful in SAP where requirements are often ambiguous and business rules are spread across multiple systems. A feature file becomes the single source of truth for business behavior.

sections.codeBlock:
@EndUserText.label: 'BDD Feature File + Step Definition'
" === Feature: Discount Calculation (Gherkin) ===
" Feature: Sales Order Discount
"   As a sales manager
"   I want to calculate discounts based on customer tier
"   So that pricing reflects customer loyalty
"
"   Background:
"     Given the discount calculator is initialized
"
"   Scenario: Apply gold tier discount
"     Given a customer with tier "GOLD"
"     When the order total is "1000"
"     Then the discount should be "200"
"
"   Scenario: Apply silver tier discount
"     Given a customer with tier "SILVER"
"     When the order total is "1000"
"     Then the discount should be "100"
"
"   Scenario: No discount for basic tier
"     Given a customer with tier "BASIC"
"     When the order total is "1000"
"     Then the discount should be "0"
"
"   Scenario: Cap discount at maximum
"     Given a customer with tier "GOLD"
"     When the order total is "10000"
"     Then the discount should be "500"
"
"   Scenario Outline: Multiple tiers
"     Given a customer with tier "<tier>"
"     When the order total is "<amount>"
"     Then the discount should be "<discount>"
"     Examples:
"       | tier   | amount | discount |
"       | GOLD   | 100    | 20       |
"       | SILVER | 100    | 10       |
"       | BASIC  | 100    | 0        |

" === Step Definition Class ===
CLASS zcl_bdd_discount_steps DEFINITION PUBLIC FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC.

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES if_bdd_step_definition.

  PRIVATE SECTION.
    DATA mo_calculator TYPE REF TO zcl_discount.
    DATA mv_tier TYPE string.
    DATA mv_amount TYPE i.
    DATA mv_result TYPE i.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_bdd_discount_steps IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD if_bdd_step_definition~initialize.
    mo_calculator = NEW zcl_discount( ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD if_bdd_step_definition~execute_step.
    CASE iv_step_type.
      WHEN 'given'.
        IF iv_step_text CS 'customer with tier'.
          mv_tier = iv_parameter.
        ENDIF.
      WHEN 'when'.
        IF iv_step_text CS 'order total is'.
          mv_amount = CONV i( iv_parameter ).
          mv_result = mo_calculator->calculate(
            iv_amount = mv_amount
            iv_tier   = mv_tier ).
        ENDIF.
      WHEN 'then'.
        IF iv_step_text CS 'discount should be'.
          DATA(lv_expected) = CONV i( iv_parameter ).
          cl_abap_unit_assert=>assert_equals(
            act = mv_result
            exp = lv_expected ).
        ENDIF.
    ENDCASE.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

organizationalDimension.challenge: How do you introduce BDD to a team that has never written automated tests of any kind?
organizationalDimension.teamStructure: Start with one 'BDD champion' per scrum team. The champion writes the feature files with the product owner in the refinement session — before development starts. The step definitions are shared across teams via a common package. A BDD community of practice meets bi-weekly to share patterns and maintain the step library.
organizationalDimension.governance: Feature files become part of the definition of done. No feature is complete until the Gherkin scenarios pass in CI. The product owner approves the feature files before development starts — if the behavior is wrong, fix the feature file, not the implementation. Step definitions are reviewed by the BDD CoP to ensure consistency.
organizationalDimension.conflictResolution: When the PO and developer disagree on expected behavior: the feature file is the arbiter. If the PO changes their mind, they update the feature file (and the test becomes red). The developer makes it green again. This is faster and less ambiguous than email threads or meeting minutes.
organizationalDimension.staffPlusTip: BDD is an organizational change more than a technical one. The hardest part is teaching product owners to write Gherkin. Start with one PO who is willing to try. Show them the feature file as a deliverable they can read. Let them see the value of unambiguous requirements expressed in their language.

sections.title: Performance & Regression Testing

description.value:
Regression testing in SAP is expensive because full integration test suites can take hours. The key is to use the test suite selection strategy: a fast feedback layer (unit + smoke, runs on every commit), a critical path layer (core business transactions, runs on PR merge), and a full suite layer (all integration + E2E, runs nightly). This is the same approach that FAANG companies use for their test suites — not everything needs to run every time.

SAT (Runtime Analysis): Transaction SAT is the ABAP performance measurement tool. It records statement-level performance data: database access time, CPU time, number of database calls, memory consumption. For performance regression testing, you can run SAT from ABAP Unit using the CL_SAT_RESULT API: run a test, measure the baseline, store it, and compare future runs to the baseline. If the new version of the code is > 2x slower than the baseline, the test fails.

Performance Regression Detection: The challenge is separating signal from noise. SAP performance varies based on system load, database cache state, and network latency. A single run is unreliable — you need statistical baselines. Run the performance test 5 times, use the median, compare against the median of the last 10 historical runs. A 20% degradation over the baseline is likely a real regression. A 200% degradation is definitely a regression.

sections.codeBlock:
@EndUserText.label: 'Performance Test with SAT Measurement'
" === Performance regression test using SAT ===
CLASS ltcl_performance_test DEFINITION FOR TESTING
  RISK LEVEL HARMLESS DURATION MEDIUM.

  PRIVATE SECTION.
    METHODS test_calculation_performance FOR TESTING.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS ltcl_performance_test IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD test_calculation_performance.
    " Start SAT measurement
    DATA(lo_sat) = cl_sat_api=>get_instance( ).
    lo_sat->start_measurement(
      iv_profile = 'Z_PERF_PROFILE'
      iv_scope   = cl_sat_api=>scope-full ).

    " Execute the code under test
    DATA(lo_calculator) = NEW zcl_order_calculator( ).
    DO 1000 TIMES.
      DATA(lv_result) = lo_calculator->calculate_discount(
        iv_order_total    = 100
        iv_customer_tier  = 'GOLD' ).
    ENDDO.

    " Stop measurement and get results
    lo_sat->stop_measurement( ).
    DATA(ls_result) = lo_sat->get_results( ).

    " Performance assertions
    cl_abap_unit_assert=>assert_between(
      act = ls_result-db_time_ms
      low = 0
      high = 500
      msg = 'Database time should be under 500ms' ).

    cl_abap_unit_assert=>assert_between(
      act = ls_result-cpu_time_ms
      low = 0
      high = 100
      msg = 'CPU time should be under 100ms' ).

    cl_abap_unit_assert=>assert_between(
      act = ls_result-db_calls
      low = 0
      high = 50
      msg = 'Should not exceed 50 DB calls' ).
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

" === Regression detection with baseline comparison ===
CLASS zcl_perf_baseline DEFINITION PUBLIC FINAL CREATE PUBLIC.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS check_regression
      IMPORTING
        iv_current_ms   TYPE i
        iv_test_name    TYPE string
      RETURNING
        VALUE(rv_ok)    TYPE abap_bool.

  PRIVATE SECTION.
    DATA mt_baselines TYPE SORTED TABLE OF zperf_baseline
      WITH UNIQUE KEY test_name.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_perf_baseline IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD check_regression.
    " Get median of last 10 runs
    SELECT MEDIAN( avg_time_ms )
      FROM zperf_results
      WHERE test_name = @iv_test_name
      ORDER BY run_date DESC
      UP TO 10 ROWS
      INTO @DATA(lv_baseline).

    IF sy-subrc <> 0.
      rv_ok = abap_true.  " First run, no baseline yet
      RETURN.
    ENDIF.

    " Allow 50% above baseline
    IF iv_current_ms > lv_baseline * 1.5.
      rv_ok = abap_false.  " Regression detected
    ELSE.
      rv_ok = abap_true.
    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

adversarialResponse.challenge: "Performance regression tests are too flaky. They fail randomly based on system load. We stopped running them in CI because of false positives."
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "Make the threshold looser." (treats symptom, not cause)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "Flaky performance tests are a known problem across all platforms — not just ABAP. Let me suggest a tiered approach: (1) Use statistical baselines, not absolute thresholds. Run the perf test 5 times, take the median, compare against the median of the last 10 runs. This filters out noise from load spikes. (2) Separate 'slow vs broken' — a perf test should never fail a build. It should produce a warning and a trend chart. If it's consistently >2x over baseline for 3 consecutive runs, escalate to the team. (3) Categorize tests by stability: Java-like microbenchmarks (SAT-based) are medium stability. Full scenario perf tests (ecatt) are low stability — these should be manual or weekly, not per-commit. (4) Implement a test quarantine: if a perf test fails 3 times in a row, it's moved to a quarantine suite and the owner is notified. This prevents the test suite from becoming unreliable while still tracking the problem."
adversarialResponse.pattern: systematic-de-flaking — acknowledge flakiness as a known issue, propose statistical approach, separate signal from noise, categorize by stability, implement quarantine mechanism.

sections.title: Staff+ Testing Strategy

description.value:
At Staff+ level, the question is not "how do I write a unit test?" but rather "what should we test, what should we not test, and how do we balance testing investment against feature delivery?" Testing strategy at this level is about test debt management — understanding the gaps in your test coverage and making deliberate decisions about where to invest.

What to Test: The industry consensus at FAANG+ level is: test everything that can break and matters. A pragmatic heuristic: (1) Business logic — test exhaustively (every branch, every edge case). (2) Data transformations — test all mappings and conversions. (3) Integration points — test the wiring, not the called system's logic. (4) Error handling — test the error paths, not the happy path only. (5) DON'T test: generated code, boilerplate, configuration files, trivial delegation, third-party library behavior.

Test Debt Management: Like technical debt, test debt accumulates when you cut corners. Track it explicitly: create a test debt backlog item per module. Classify: critical (untested business logic with high change frequency), moderate (untested but stable code), low (utility code with low risk). Dedicate 20% of each sprint to reducing critical test debt. The metric to watch is not coverage percentage but test coverage of changed code — are your tests covering the code that actually changes?

Testing ROI: Every test has a cost: writing time, maintenance time, CI execution time, flakiness investigation time. The ROI calculation is: ROI = (Defects Caught × Cost Per Defect) / (Test Creation Cost + Test Maintenance Cost). A test that catches 5 production defects per year is high ROI. A test that never catches a defect and fails once per month due to flakiness is negative ROI. Remove or replace the low-ROI tests.

sections.codeBlock:
@EndUserText.label: 'Staff+ Testing Governance Framework'
" === Test Debt Register ===
"    Module         | Risk | Coverage | Change Freq | Test Debt | Priority
"    Z_SALES_ORDER  | HIGH | 92%      | HIGH        | LOW       | MONITOR
"    Z_PRICING      | HIGH | 45%      | HIGH        | HIGH      | Q1 TARGET
"    Z_INVENTORY    | MED  | 70%      | LOW         | MED       | BACKLOG
"    Z_LOGGER       | LOW  | 15%      | LOW         | LOW       | IGNORE

" === Test Debt Backlog Query ===
CLASS zcl_test_debt_analyzer DEFINITION PUBLIC FINAL CREATE PUBLIC.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS analyze_test_debt
      IMPORTING
        iv_package     TYPE devclass
      RETURNING
        VALUE(rt_debt) TYPE ztest_debt_tab.

  PRIVATE SECTION.
    METHODS calculate_risk
      IMPORTING
        iv_change_freq TYPE i
      RETURNING
        VALUE(rv_risk) TYPE string.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_test_debt_analyzer IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD analyze_test_debt.
    " For each object in package, assess:
    " - Coverage (from SCOV)
    " - Change frequency (from gCTS commit history)
    " - Criticality (from incident data)
    " - Calculate priority: CRIT × (1 - coverage) × change_freq

    SELECT FROM z_dev_objects AS obj
      LEFT JOIN scov_coverage AS cov
        ON obj~object_name = cov~object_name
      LEFT JOIN zgit_commit_stats AS git
        ON obj~object_name = git~object_name
      FIELDS
        obj~object_name,
        cov~statement_pct AS coverage,
        git~commit_count_90d AS change_frequency,
        obj~incident_count_90d AS incident_count
      WHERE obj~package = @iv_package
      INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_analysis).

    LOOP AT lt_analysis INTO DATA(ls_obj).
      DATA(ls_debt) = VALUE ztest_debt(
        object_name   = ls_obj-object_name
        coverage_pct  = ls_obj-coverage
        change_freq   = ls_obj-change_frequency
        incident_count = ls_obj-incident_count
        risk          = calculate_risk( ls_obj-incident_count )
        test_debt     = COND #(
          WHEN ls_obj-coverage < 50 AND ls_obj-change_frequency > 10
          THEN 'HIGH'
          ELSE 'LOW' ) ).
      APPEND ls_debt TO rt_debt.
    ENDLOOP.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD calculate_risk.
    IF iv_change_freq > 20.
      rv_risk = 'CRITICAL'.
    ELSEIF iv_change_freq > 10.
      rv_risk = 'HIGH'.
    ELSE.
      rv_risk = 'MEDIUM'.
    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

" === Testing ROI Calculator ===
METHOD calculate_testing_roi.
  " Inputs
  DATA(lv_defect_cost) = 5000.      " Cost per production defect ($)
  DATA(lv_defects_prev) = 12.       " Defects caught by tests in 6 months
  DATA(lv_write_hours) = 40.        " Hours to write tests
  DATA(lv_maint_hours) = 8.         " Hours per month maintaining tests
  DATA(lv_hourly_rate) = 150.       " Developer hourly rate ($)

  " ROI calculation
  DATA(lv_benefit) = lv_defect_cost * lv_defects_prev.          " $60K
  DATA(lv_cost) = ( lv_write_hours + lv_maint_hours * 6 ) * lv_hourly_rate.  " $13.2K
  DATA(lv_roi) = ( lv_benefit - lv_cost ) / lv_cost * 100.
  " Result: ~355% ROI over 6 months
ENDMETHOD.

decisionTree.prompt: How should I allocate my test budget across unit, integration, and system tests?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "What is the change frequency of this module?" High-change modules need comprehensive unit tests. Stable modules need integration tests for critical paths only.

branches.condition: High change frequency (weekly or daily), business-critical logic
branches.path: Invest 70% of test budget in unit tests. Maintain 90%+ branch coverage. Use integration tests only for API boundary validation. System tests are risk-based (critical scenarios only).

branches.condition: Medium change frequency (monthly), stable business logic
branches.path: Invest 50% in unit tests (core paths only), 30% in integration tests (API contracts), 20% in system tests (key user journeys). Coverage target: 70% statement.

branches.condition: Low change frequency (quarterly or less), utility or infrastructure
branches.path: Invest 90% in integration tests. Unit tests are optional — write them only for complex internal logic. System tests are not needed. Coverage target: 50% statement.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: Your test budget is not infinite. Every test you write is a maintenance liability. The highest ROI investment is testing the code that changes most often and has the highest business impact. Use the change frequency × criticality matrix to prioritize.

details: Staff+ Insight: Testing at FAANG scale is not about achieving a coverage number — it's about making confident decisions. Can you deploy on Friday? That depends on your test coverage of changed code and the speed of your regression suite. The best ABAP teams I've seen have: (1) unit tests for all business logic with 90%+ branch coverage, (2) integration tests for every API contract, (3) a 15-minute CI pipeline that gives confident pass/fail, (4) performance regression detection, (5) explicit test debt management with sprint allocation. Testing is not a phase. It is how you do development.
